flashcards.front: What is a Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a data structure in which each node has at most two children and each node represents a value, with all values in the left subtree being less than the node's value and all values in the right subtree being greater.

flashcards.front: What are the properties of a Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: Each node has a unique value, all values in the left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in the right subtree are greater than the node's value.

flashcards.front: What is the average time complexity of search, insert, and delete operations in a Binary Search Tree?
What is the difference between a full Binary Search Tree and an empty Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: A full Binary Search Tree is a BST in which every node has two children, while an empty Binary Search Tree is a BST with no nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the disadvantage of using a Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: A Binary Search Tree can become unbalanced, leading to poor performance (worst-case time complexity of O(n)).

flashcards.front: How does a Binary Search Tree compare to a Hash Table in terms of search, insert, and delete operations?
flashcards.back: A Binary Search Tree has an average time complexity of O(log n), while a Hash Table has an average time complexity of O(1).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the root node in a Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: The root node is the topmost node in a Binary Search Tree and serves as the starting point for search, insert, and delete operations.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the leaf node in a Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: A leaf node is a node with no children and represents a value in the Binary Search Tree.
